YES! Ask and you shall receive. SO EXCITED to review this—anything but HOHUM.
This had everything I could ask for in a Sunday Puzzle. Fresh, innovative idea that’s a challenge for both constructor and solver—and one that contributes to the solve once you get it but still isn’t a lay-up. The theme entries are smooth as silk and great accessory fill as well—PROMPOSAL, AIRHIGHFIVE (what you give after the proposal is accepted?) GOESROGUE, HOLYCANNOLI. (Good choice not to crowd this grid and the double themer in the middle is masterful). Just about the right difficulty of clues for me—an occasional easy clue to get over the tricky spots but plenty to stump over. And a few clever misdirections—PRISM, COSTAR, SCALP. Even a sly connection between Ned Flanders and Bouvier DES Flandres. I am so sorry that folks solving on the magazine had an issue and I hope they got to solve it elsewhere, that’s unforgivable for a such a wonderful puzzle.
I had an interesting path to get the theme—had the rebus at ISLANDHOPPED but had PLAYEDHARD so had DEAD but not HEAT—figured the heat might come from the nuclear fusion! Then WHATATREAT before IFEELGREAT—which seemed to work with Pizza HUT. SECRETPLOTS finally broke it open.
One minor nit and one observation—when you PAWN an item you are officially lending it for security on a loan, not selling it. And last, our British friends would argue that the answer to most words ending in “ize” is MISSPELLED.

My man Mesmer, in his day was like one of those celebrity Hollywood doctors who get exposed once in a while as a fraud, but not before socking away $100 million. In Paris, Mesmer treated groups of patients using a device called a baquet (a tub filled with magnetized water and iron rods). People sat around it holding rods or ropes as mood music played in the background, often glass harmonicas, and in these group hypnosis theatricals his patients would frequently go into convulsions, trances, and panicked outbursts. But hypnosis was secondary to his theory of why such behavior occurred -- animal magnetism, an invisible force that flowed through all things and which Mesmer thought could be manipulated, principally by using magnets in creative ways. So his contribution to history was just a side effect, based on his innate understanding of human suggestibility, of his debunked life's work.

A refreshingly tough Sunday!

First, the trick. My experience was similar to a few Thursdays of years past, in that I partially “figured it out” early, but only *actually* figured it out late — and the long swath in between was maddening. 

In this case, I realized early on that I would need to drop certain letters from certain across entries in order to make things work. How the letters got dropped felt somewhat random, and I went through all sorts of false beliefs about what dictated it (the borrowed letter being shoved above or below? duplicate letters being fused? pure chaos?!!) as I dutifully flogged through. Along the way, of course, I felt irritated by what I perceived as “bad clues” to some vertical entries (items in Ace are HARD? a near tie is DEAD? a final effort is a GASP? Ok, sure, maybe in some looser idiomatic sense, but come on!). It was only when I decided to actually enter my dropped letters in rebus form (for bookkeeping) that I realized how the two intersected, and sped through all the theme answers.

But second, I just feel like this Sunday had properly challenging clues in a way that I haven’t seen in some time. That whole section around WAPITI crossing PALISADE sent me on so many false starts, as did the section around BRANCA crossing METRO and SIERRA. Felt so gratifying when I cracked em!

Lovely touches in the making of this puzzle:
•	The six fused phrases (such as DEAD HEAT and YARD CARE) are all, elegantly, the same length.
•	Their first letters are different, as well as their last letters.  So, DeaD HeaT works, but DeaD HeaD doesn’t because its last letters are the same.  More elegance.
•	The across answers with rebuses no only fit grid symmetry, but are in-the-language, most of them colorful.
•	On top of this, there are 15 NYT debut answers – 15! – all interesting and unforced, such as CRÈME EGG, AIR HIGH FIVE, HOLY CANNOLI, PROMPOSAL, and I FEEL GREAT.

This theme was, for me, a tough nut to crack.  Thorny cluing slowed me up, and while I knew there was a rebus, I wasn’t sure, for quite a while, which square held it.  And thus, my brain, which lives for nutcracking moments, was in heaven.

I've always assumed that it was a question of experience.  If you've never encountered a rebus puzzle before, the first time you do your encounter is going to be frustrating and annoying.  "What the heck is going on here?  Why is the constructor ruining my fun?"

It's very rare that I truly dislike a puzzle, and I do appreciate the work that goes into them, but this was not enjoyable.

The theme makes no sense whatsoever. There is no central idea tying it together. It feels completely random, and completely unfair.

First, what relation does "nuclear" have to the puzzle? A nucleus is at the exact center of an atom. You would expect the rebuses, then, to be at the exact center of the across entries. They were not. 49A had four letters off to the left of the rebus, and six to the right. That is not the "center."

Second, the letters in the rebuses are completely random. Going back to the "nuclear" in the title, I thought maybe the rebuses would be atomic symbols. Nope. What about the letters in between the rebuses in the down entries? Nope. It's nonsense. Gibberish.

This was a trick, not a theme. We were given no way to deduce where the rebuses would fall, or -- more importantly -- WHY these specific letters were rebuses at all. The misleading title of the puzzle made it more confusing. I was hunting for meaning where there was none.

Internal monologue: "Nuclear fusion. Ok, so some letters in the middle are likely to be fused. Got it. ISLANDOPPED. Cool -- the D and the H are fused. Not sure why 24D is DEAD, though. Anyway, so they're fused when you pronounce them, kinda. Sorta. Do I really pronounce the H separately when I say island hopped? Huh. Not sure. OK. On to IFEELREAT. I guess a desperate final effort could be LAST. If I give it my all, couldn't I give it my LAST? But wait -- I definitely pronounce the G if I'm saying I feel great. What the hell is going on here?"

I finally figured it out with YARDCARE because I *knew* CINDERBLOCK had to be right, somehow. Somehow.

I was already mostly done with the puzzle at that point, so I spent the last minute laughing at what an idiot I was while I was filling in all the little hash marks in the special squares.

That was fun.

Dear Will Shortz: 
Dude.
Dude, can I just have a puzzle that doesn't require me looking up addenda and holding the grid up to the mirror and getting out a cryptography manual? A Sunday puzzle with clues and responses and a gentle little clever gimmick? 
Today's puzzle is an absolute brick wall to me, and I've been solving  the NYT for 40 years. Eugene T. Maleska would have never perpetrated such torture, I can tell ya that. 
The phrase that appears in the composer's bio is quite telling. It says, "Good puzzlemakers are always pushing boundaries."

Sure, I've always enjoyed a gimmick, but not if that gimmick is too much work. After all, it's already a challenge to find out what the answers are. That's what I enjoy doing when I sit down  in the morning sun with my morning tea and my radish-tofu smoothie. 
I know that some solvers enjoyed today's puzzle, and can anticipate that they land on the other side of the innovation question. To them I say "EXEUTI MII FROY LATA GHERKIN." They'll figure it out.

I've read some comments. I've done some thinking. And I've come to the conclusion: It's not a bad puzzle, just a bad title. "Nuclear Fusion" does not work for either the across or down themers.

First, there are two types of "fusion" in the grid: the rebuses, which fuse two letters into one box; and the doubled letters in the down entries.

The across entries are fused by these rebuses. It's the first and most obvious "fusion" I saw in the grid. But they are not fused via their nuclei. In atomic terms, they're more of a covalent bond, with the end of one word fused to the beginning of the next.

The down entries do share a nucleus, that being the doubled letters at their center: the Es and As at the middle of DEAD HEAT, for example. But they are also fused at the top and bottom, via the rebuses. The D/H and D/T are just as fused, visually, as the Es and As. Every letter is fused, not just the ones in the nucleus.

The idea of fused nuclei just does not work. It created a huge amount of confusion and frustration on my part. Even after reading the column, even after commenters explained the trick to me, it still did not work.

The puzzle needed a different title, or no title at all. That would have made it far less frustrating. Trying to fit the theme to the title felt like trying to put a square peg in a round hole.

I get it, this is hard for a new solver. But consider it a learning experience. And later, when you are not a new solver anymore, you will appreciate the challenge and the excitement of being able to solve it without hints, and be disappointed when they are giving to you when you might have solved it yourself.

@Nate Jones -- Definitely a learned skill. My advice -- which I followed today: stick to the answers you know are right in the non-rebus squares. You'll eventually see what the answers *should* be including the rebus squares (ISLAND HOPPED, for example). Fill in the letters that fit with a cross and leave the others blank. Come up with a hypothesis as to what the trick might be. (The title helped today.) See if it applies to more than one of the theme entries. If it doesn't, think of another. Eventually it'll click into place.

@Nate Jones circles and highlights are typical of early-week puzzles, which are intentionally easier. As a general rule each puzzle gets harder from Monday-Saturday. That includes more misdirection in clues and fewer visual indicators in the grid. Sundays are a bit of a wild card. Because the grid is bigger many of the clues and answers are easier than Friday or Saturday (so the puzzle doesn’t take all day), but not all are, and variations such as rebuses appear fairly frequently. As you become more experienced you will come to enjoy having fewer “helpers” later in the week, as it makes the puzzles more interesting for seasoned solvers. (That, or you’ll decide the late-week puzzles aren’t for you, which is perfectly fine,)

commentBody:
@Amanda Nace
Oh, Amanda! Tough start to your visit to CrossLandia.
On many Thursdays and some Sundays, the crossword gets even trickier than usual. Instead of one letter per square, it will force you to squish two or more letters in. This is called a "rebus" here -- not its usual English meaning.

So, to pick one of your stumbling blocks, the solution to 23A is indeed ISLANDHOPPED but that's one letter too many for the spaces allotted. So something's gotta give.

Looking at the Down clues crossing 23A, you'll see that it's the DH pair that's the trouble. You're going to have to put both letters in one square.

Click on the square you want to squish in. Look for an icon or a menu entry that refers to REBUS. Click on that, a small box will appear, type DH in there (not HD, please!), click on REBUS again. And there you are, your regular grid square should now contain two letters.

There are more answers in this puzzle that are rebuses. Keep looking for answers that seem to be missing letters and try the rebus trick. 

Good luck and keep us posted. The commentariat is anxiously waiting for your next bulletin.

I remember how confusing it was the first time I encountered a rebus puzzle....  But to me, I just knew I wasn't understanding how the puzzle worked. I didn't blame the constructor or the puzzle, or act like someone was doing something bad to me. Those are the reactions that I truly cannot comprehend.  I simply knew this was an element somehow that I didn't understand yet.

I couldn't complete the puzzle, so I came to the column and the comments, and came to understand what I was missing. And probably because I didn't spew hatred or accusations toward the puzzle or a constructor or the world for letting me down, kind commenters out here explained not only how they work, but how to look for them and when I should be suspicious. 

It helped me immensely, and I've enjoyed every single one since. I get a certain glee about them! I love the aha!  It took practice to be able to solve every single one without help, but I more and more built up those skills.

I first encountered rebuses in the crossword sense of the term in Shortz Sunday Puzzle collections. I was very puzzled the first time but then saw little pictures in the answer key. I think they were pumpkins.

I was afraid they would disappear when the puzzles came online and was happy to learn that a somewhat satisfactory workaround was devised. I do miss drawing little pumpkins, though.

Only once have I really gotten angry at a rebus. One Sunday I solved the puzzle in my usual time and then spent over 2 more hours trying to get them to work. I think that was in my first long streak and I was determined not to let it go because of a purely technical issue.

@Seán H this is, for better or worse, not a glitch. It’s how the rebuses are designed to work. If you put just the first letter of any rebus in, it counts as correct. This setup makes solving rebuses more straightforward. Some of us don’t like it, but it is currently consistent with the way all rebus puzzles work on this app.

If you want all of your rebuses to be entered correctly in future, the moment you realize it’s a rebus puzzle, erase one square that is not a rebus. This will let you ensure that the final square you fill in is *not* a rebus square and will give you the opportunity to fill in all of the rebuses correctly without the first letter of the last one triggering a completed puzzle.

"The app" accepts the first letter of a rebus. If you want to have all of the rebuses appear correctly, follow the advice of long time commenter David Connell: leave a non-rebus square unfilled until you have filled in all the rebuses.
